speaker: interviewer
text: The date is July 18th, 1995. The survivor is Jean Melzer. Fink was the name at birth. Interviewer is Atara Hirsch. We're in Brooklyn, New York, USA. Language is English.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's July 18, 1995. The survivor is Jean Melzer. Fink was the name at birth. Atara Hirsch is the interviewer. Brooklyn, New York, USA. Language is English. OK. Tell us your name and spell that for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Jean Melzer, J-E-A-N M-E-L-Z-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was your name at birth, your maiden name, and can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: F-I-N-K, Fink.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Do you have any other names, any nicknames, anything like that? No. OK. What's your birth date? And how old are you?

speaker: interviewee
text: May 10, 1923. 72, 73-- I don't know, who count? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: May 10. It's over.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Why don't you tell us a little about your prewar life, where you were born.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Zawalow. And I had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Z-A-W-A-L-O-W. We had a very comfortable home. I had a wonderful childhood. Everything was very good for us. My parents had a business, grocery. And I had a younger brother. And we, we lived very good there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Until-- we used to have together-- to belong to organizations, to go on all kinds of-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to get together from other villages, from other cities. And we had a wonderful life until the war start. And anti-Semitism start a little bit before the war. We first lived out of the town. And they made us to leave this house. And we had to go into the center of the town. We bought another house. And we continued with the business. They used to break our windows.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me first-- about what year are we talking, that, that you had to leave your house? When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 193-- '36 or '37.

speaker: interviewer
text: Put the papers down. Can you? OK. OK. So this was in 1937? So you were, you were a student. How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm--

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I was like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 12 or 13 years. I was then in another town and school. Because in our town, it wasn't-- I finished in our town. It was a small town. So I went to another town to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: To school there. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I came home for the vacation, so I was told how it was, that the, the windows were broken. And they sent us a note that we have to leave the town till-- the house. Till three days, we had to leave it. My parents bought another house in the center of the town. And we continued with the, with the life. It wasn't-- it was still very, very good till 1939, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the real war start.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I want to backtrack for a minute to before the war. Just tell me a little about your community. And did you belong to a synagogue? Were you observant?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. We were observers of the holidays, all the holidays. We had a kosher home. We, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we belonged to organizations, to the Zionist groups. We had, we had, like, rented a, a place. And all the youth used to come there. And we were there, making all kind of speeches. And, and we had a very happy life there like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had how many siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: One.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about your siblings.

speaker: interviewee
text: One brother, a younger one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was how old?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was three years younger than I am.

speaker: interviewer
text: Three years, so--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was 14 years when he was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that that he was killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was he killed in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 19-- '41 or '43. Can I take the paper? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE] glasses.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so around 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was 14.

speaker: interviewee
text: '40-- '41, '41, [INAUDIBLE], yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So tell me a little about your parents. What were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was [? Joseph. ?] And my mother's name was Rachela, Rachela.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gottesman. He was a successful businessman. We were-- and they would always working, busy. And we continued school, playing, going with our friends, were Gentiles. And we were very friendly, very-- when a holiday was a Jewish or a-- we invited them. We played together. And we always were very friendly. We went to school together, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] until, until the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when did you first start to feel anti-Semitism in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '36, it all-- it was all starting, like-- but, like, we were young. And we still didn't feel so. Because the parents felt more. And we still lived, till '39, very happily. And in '39, when the war real start, then it begin to be worst. We, we had to give up some kind of business. Our friends started to be a little bit different to us. But still, we continued our, our life normally.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you went to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the school that you went to. Was it a yeshiva?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I went to a regular school, a public school, and-- like high school. And afternoon, I used to go to [? Bes ?] [? Yankov ?]. So I was teached both in Jewish, in Jewish [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] history and, and in there [? words ?] education. The Jewish people were always for education. I remember in our home, we always had private teachers. And I studied German even, because we, we used to love the German language. And we used to think that they are such a intelligent-- no, not nature. How would you say-- intelligent people that it cannot be that they shall do this, what we heard when Hitler start coming. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] when-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then I went, when I finished high school, I start studying the bookkeeping. And I went to school in [INAUDIBLE]. But I was a short time there. And I had to come back home, because it was very bad when the Germans [INAUDIBLE] that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That's still before the war, right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before the war-- that was in '39? What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '39, we were in Poland, yeah. We were all the time in Poland. I mean--

speaker: interviewer
text: No. When did you go to the bookkeeping school? What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Don't ?] [? recall. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. It will be, like, in '39-- mhm, '39, '40, '39. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] My-- we had family there in the city. We were surrounded always with friends, aunts, uncles. My mother had three brothers and two sisters. They lived in other towns. But my father had two sisters. They lived in the same town. I had cousins and we-- and friends there. We were very happy together.

speaker: interviewer
text: You used to get together with your family on the holidays and all that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe any holiday that sticks out in your mind that you used to get together with any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, like Passover, we used to have together. My, my father had two sisters. So at one time, yeah, we were living already in the center of the town there, where we bought another house. And it was Passover, that the stores were closed. And at this time, we had a house there that we rented out to another, to a Gentile. And a policeman was living there. And they came to break the windows. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who came, the Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was still the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Poles.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- and instead to break our window, because it was closed, like, with locks. Because when the store was closed and the entrance in this place was closed, because it was a holiday. So those windows were to the open from this apartment. They broke his window. And he ran out. And he catched one. It was a Ukranian. And then they-- this was still in time when Poland was so-- that's when we start feeling the, the war is near there, like when Hitler came to the-- in 1933. So everything used to change.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was this incident in?

speaker: interviewee
text: So on the year that they broke the windows. And we were not touched then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: But we were scared. And it interrupted our seder, that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we felt it's not-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that maybe we, we have to try to, to leave this Poland, to run. But then we start again. There were-- there, there are our roots. Our grandparents will, will lived there. And all our families are there. Why shall we leave? But we, we felt that we need a Jewish, a land that we shall have where to go to. That's when we were belonging to the organizations. We, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we tried to collect money for Israel and all those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents think about leaving and going to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to Israel-- my parents were thinking of going to the United States. Because my mother and father were-- when they were single, they were in the United States. They came back before the, the war start, the first war, 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did they seriously talk about leaving after 1933?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not so seriously, but they were thinking of it. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your town like? How large of a Jewish community was there in Zawalow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the town of the Jewish community wasn't big. It was small. We had, like, 30 Jewish families. The rest was everything Gentile. It was a small town. But we lived very friendly with the, the Gentile people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have, I had friends. And we grew up together. When, when they had a holiday, they invited us. We couldn't participate in everything what they do on according to our kosher. So we ate fruit or other things. And they were invited to our holidays. We lived always friendly with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived in a house or an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in a house. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe the house a little?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The house was in the center of the town. We had a store. And it had, like, four entrances. In the front were two entrances, one to the store, the other one to our house-- to the building, to the room, which was only three rooms. But the rooms were very large. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And on the other side was one entrance. And in the back was an entrance. And, of course, a magazine or a-- where we kept from the store all those things-- how do you call it? Magazine? From the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: Magazine. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Receipts?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not receipts-- products, like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we had, let's say, you bags of rice--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, the inventory.

speaker: interviewee
text: Flour, this very--

speaker: interviewer
text: Inventory.

speaker: interviewee
text: The inventory? Whatever it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you share a room with your brother in that house? Or did you have your own room?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, we had-- no, I didn't have my own room in the beginning. We had-- because we rented. When we bought the house, this policeman was there living. So that's why we couldn't. And we tried, he shall move out. And then we had the other rooms, too. But me and my brother had a separate- like, it was divided, like a separate room. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your brother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Zalman, Zalman, a Jewish name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the policeman-- you got along with the Gentile policeman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Yeah. Yeah. When he heard, he ran out right away. He ran-- he thought-- we were screaming. We heard such a noise. So-- and the policeman thought they, they in the store. He ran in through the house to the store and opened the door and ran after them. And he, he catched them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember when year that was, around what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, maybe '37.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was the first time you started to realize how bad things were getting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then how did it go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: But it still went in. A lot of, they said, don't buy by Jews and don't go to-- with Jews. But it quiet down again. And we had our business still up. And then we lived OK till '39.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in '39 also, when the war start, the-- Poland was like-- the Germans came in. And the Russians came in. So we were till 1941, until the, until the Russians, which was also OK. The-- it wasn't as good as it used to be before the war start. But we, we continued in living normally until '41, when the war start. Will you take this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: I must check.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just tell me. So how did your life change fro '39 to '41? Did you go into a ghetto or anything like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was it you're asking?

speaker: interviewer
text: From '39 to 41, tell me a little about how your life changed once the Russians came in.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the Russians came in, we were considered as that we are, we are rich. My father owned land. And we were storekeepers. So they, they were more for, Like, you should be poorer people. But still, we, we didn't have no more our store. And because my father gave up the store, we had a lot of land. We--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you put the paper down, just because it makes a little bit of noise, just maybe on the chair over there? OK. I'm sorry. So what happened to the store-- you were telling us?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to then-- in our store, they put up another store, and not a grocery no more. And we worked there, as working people.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Russians did this? Who did this? Who took away the store?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they didn't take it away. But we discontinued the grocery. And we just had, the like, other, other things. The, the Russians took the-- and put like-- it was-- that was-- I really don't want to go into this.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Can we start from this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, we can go on. So tell me, tell me-- we don't have to talk about the store then. Tell me what happened in '41.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? From ?] Russia. Yeah. Let's leave.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we don't have to-- we don't talk about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] Russia with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't, don't even have to get-- you know, you don't need your paper. We can talk about '41. Tell me about what happened then. You're doing fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: When we were in-- at home in Zawalow, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when the Germans came in, we had-- they took all those people to work. And the first day, when they just came, they started to ask where the Jewish people lived. And the Gentiles, the Polish people who were there, they right away brought them into the Jewish places where they were. They were very happy to see them. They-- when they came in, they were happy. They screamed, [? Hora ?], [? Hora ?], to see them. And they showed right away the Jewish places. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They start taking us to work, to all kind of work. I had three houses away from us, a friend who went to school together with me. And he joined the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a Gentile?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, [? Romich. ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He joined the Nazis a little later. But he was pro them right away. And he, he took my father to work, and other people. They cut off their beards. And they, they took them to, to ditch, like street work, to do all kinds of dirty works. They took them, like when it was lot rain or mud, they had to lay down, put them on the floor, and get up, lay down, and get up, just to humiliate them. They shall be-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] and beaten. And they took them to dig stones. We have-- like our city was-- now I go back to this, how the city was. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They had-- it was surrounded like with-- it was in a, in a vale, or around was hills. The hills had stones. So we had to go dig those stones. They took the men specially. And they beat them. And they took them there to work. He, this [? Romich, ?] specially, he was very bad to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they only took the men? What did they do with the women at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The women also was taking to-- took to work. But they took them, like, to do their houseworks, to wash their clothes, to, to clean toilets-- they-- and-- by everything, they were beating us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did they take you and your mother to do housework?

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, yeah, specially the young ones more than my mother. They used to took, to take us to field work that-- daily. And this work was unnecessarily. But they took us. Because they wanted just to humiliate us and to, to beat us, and to make us work for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who did, who did you work for specifically? Did you work for a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, when they, not-- they had, like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they had-- they-- like-- what was it? The, the, like city hall. And that, they took us to clean out those houses and put them privately where they were living.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your mother-- what did she do during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- my mother was at home. Her, they didn't take to this work at this time, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was able to stay in your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother was taken to work, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was he taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Also to this kind of work, [INAUDIBLE] they-- to sweep the streets, to, to wash, to wash the, the sidewalks, the-- such things.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So we're going to now talk about after '41. This was until '41, right? And then we're going to go on now.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in '41 when--
